input: Choose the correct option (A–D): In the described setup, what combination of training, aggregation, policy properties, SLA guarantees, data heterogeneity, and inference correctly characterizes the outcome? [A] Local DDPG training with weighted aggregation at the near-RT RIC yields privacy-preserving but not generalizable policies that maintain only eMBB throughput SLAs under i.i.d conditions and use high-latency inference at the non-RT RIC. [B] Centralized PPO training with raw data sharing yields policies that violate URLLC delay SLAs and rely on offline, high-latency inference at the near-RT RIC. [C] MVNOs locally train DDPG-based bandwidth allocation policies and share model updates for weighted aggregation at a non-RT RIC, producing privacy-preserving, generalizable RAN slicing policies that maintain URLLC delay and eMBB throughput SLAs under heterogeneous non-i.i.d conditions while enabling low-latency online inference at the near-RT RIC. [D] Federated supervised learning with gradient averaging at the core network generalizes only under i.i.d data, fails to maintain SLAs, and performs inference at a far-RT controller.

Federated deep reinforcement learning that centralizes raw data at the near-RT RIC and performs unweighted aggregation produces non–privacy-preserving RAN slicing policies that generalize only under i.i.d conditions, fail to maintain URLLC delay and eMBB throughput SLAs, and require high-latency online inference at the non-RT RIC.

This setup produces privacy-preserving and generalizable RAN slicing policies that maintain _________(URLLC delay and eMBB throughput/URLLC reliability and mMTC connection density) SLAs under heterogeneous non-i.i.d conditions while enabling low-latency online inference at the near-RT RIC, with MVNOs locally training DDPG-based bandwidth allocation policies and sharing model updates for weighted aggregation at a non-RT RIC.

Assertion (A): Federated deep reinforcement learning in which MVNOs locally train DDPG-based bandwidth allocation policies and share model updates for weighted aggregation at a non-RT RIC produces privacy-preserving and generalizable RAN slicing policies that maintain URLLC delay and eMBB throughput SLAs under heterogeneous non-i.i.d conditions while enabling low-latency online inference at the near-RT RIC. Reason (R): The approach requires homogeneous i.i.d conditions across MVNOs and performs centralized training at the near-RT RIC.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Under what conditions can a browser-based volunteer distributed computing setup viably execute embarrassingly parallel urgent workloads across diverse devices without installing software on clients? [A] When it relies on peer-to-peer coordination among tabs without WebWorkers and restricts cross-origin communication. [B] When it uses Service Workers and offline caching with a decentralized task manager. [C] When it employs WebWorkers in conjunction with a centralized cross-origin task manager so tasks run in the browser across heterogeneous platforms without client-side installation. [D] Only when devices share identical hardware and a native client is preinstalled.

Browser-based volunteer distributed computing requires client-side software installation and homogeneous platforms to execute embarrassingly parallel urgent workloads, even if WebWorkers and a centralized cross-origin task manager are used.

In executing urgent, embarrassingly parallel workloads via browser-based volunteer distributed computing across heterogeneous platforms, the approach operates _________(without client-side software installation/with mandatory client-side software installation) when using WebWorkers and a centralized cross-origin task manager.

Assertion (A): Browser-based volunteer distributed computing using WebWorkers and a centralized cross-origin task manager can viably execute embarrassingly parallel urgent workloads without client-side software installation across heterogeneous platforms. Reason (R): This is because such workloads require homogeneous hardware and preinstalled client applications.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which scenario best matches how session dwell times affect task completion and overall performance in web-based volunteer computing? [A] Long and short sessions contribute equally to task completion, and performance is unaffected by session composition, eliminating overhead. [B] Most tasks are finished by the majority of sessions regardless of dwell time, so performance depends on non-value sessions and value sessions add overhead. [C] Most tasks are finished by a small minority of long dwell time sessions, many short sessions finish none, performance hinges on value sessions, and non-value sessions add overhead. [D] Short sessions finish most tasks, long sessions rarely contribute, and performance hinges on non-value sessions with no overhead.

In web-based volunteer computing, most tasks are completed by many short sessions, long dwell time sessions contribute little, performance is independent of value sessions, and non-value sessions do not impose overhead.

In web-based volunteer computing, performance is _________(dependent/independent) on value sessions because most tasks are completed by a small minority of long dwell time sessions while many short sessions complete none, and non-value sessions impose overhead.

Assertion (A): In web-based volunteer computing, most tasks are completed by a small minority of long dwell time sessions, many short sessions complete none, performance depends on value sessions, and non-value sessions impose overhead. Reason (R): Task completion is uniformly distributed across session dwell times and non-value sessions are rare.
output: A is True, R is False

input: When browsing sessions are volatile, using checkpointing partial results, batching tasks, and asynchronously prefetching new tasks increases useful work without increasing server-side load.

Assertion (A): Using checkpointing partial results, batching tasks, and asynchronously prefetching new tasks increases the amount of useful work completed during volatile browsing sessions while increasing server-side load. Reason (R): They reduce server-side load.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Tuning task granularity is unnecessary because smaller tasks both reduce task management and communication overheads and decrease the likelihood of value sessions.
Smaller tasks both raise the likelihood of value sessions and heighten task management and communication overheads, which makes tuning task granularity _________(necessary/trivial).

Assertion (A): Tuning task granularity is essential because smaller tasks increase the likelihood of value sessions but also amplify task management and communication overheads. Reason (R): Smaller tasks reduce both task management and communication overheads without affecting value sessions.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Select the statement that correctly captures both communication effects and computation sensitivity for browser-executed workloads relative to native code. [A] Data-bound workloads see WebSockets outperform XMLHttpRequest, and compute-bound kernels are highly sensitive to protocol while independent of algorithm and browser engine optimizations. [B] Protocol choice is irrelevant for both data-bound and compute-bound workloads, and performance differences versus native code arise solely from browser engine optimizations. [C] For data-bound workloads, XMLHttpRequest can outperform WebSockets, and compute-bound kernels are relatively insensitive to protocol choice, with their browser-executed performance relative to native code depending on algorithm characteristics and browser engine optimizations. [D] Data-bound workloads may favor XMLHttpRequest, but compute-bound kernels are sensitive to protocol and achieve parity with native code whenever the browser is optimized.

Compute-bound kernels are relatively _________(insensitive/sensitive) to protocol choice, and their browser-executed performance relative to native code depends on algorithm characteristics and browser engine optimizations, while for data-bound workloads XMLHttpRequest can outperform WebSockets.

Assertion (A): For data-bound workloads, XMLHttpRequest can outperform WebSockets, whereas compute-bound kernels are relatively insensitive to protocol choice and their browser-executed performance relative to native code depends on algorithm characteristics and browser engine optimizations. Reason (R): WebSockets invariably outperform XMLHttpRequest for data-bound workloads, and compute-bound kernels’ browser performance relative to native code is independent of algorithm characteristics and browser engine optimizations.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement best summarizes the delay-embedding topology contrast in Z24 Bridge natural frequency analysis? [A] Raw series form a spherical shell driven by diurnal temperature cycles, while cointegrated residuals form a torus associated with colored noise. [B] Raw series form a torus induced by cyclic temperature, while cointegrated residuals form an open ball consistent with approximately Gaussian white noise. [C] Raw series and cointegrated residuals both form open balls, but only the residuals are approximately Gaussian. [D] Raw series and cointegrated residuals both form tori, reflecting persistent periodicity in both.

In the Z24 Bridge natural frequency data, delay embeddings of the raw series are open-ball and characteristic of approximately Gaussian white-noise processes, while embeddings of cointegrated residuals are toroidal due to cyclic temperature effects.

Assertion (A): Time-delay embeddings of Z24 Bridge natural frequency time series are toroidal due to cyclic temperature effects, while embeddings of cointegrated residuals are open-ball and consistent with approximately Gaussian white-noise processes. Reason (R): The toroidal topology is caused by measurement noise, and the residuals preserve strong periodicity after cointegration.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Under a nonlinear temperature dependence of the second natural frequency, which complete statement about residual topology and the comparative effectiveness of cointegration methods is correct? [A] Linear cointegration eliminates residual topology, and Gaussian process-based nonlinear cointegration works best when the nonlinear regime is excluded from training. [B] Linear cointegration leaves no residual topological structure, and Gaussian process-based nonlinear cointegration trained on data including the nonlinear regime offers no additional benefit. [C] Linear cointegration leaves residual topological structure, whereas Gaussian process-based nonlinear cointegration trained on data that include the nonlinear regime more effectively removes these effects. [D] Residual topology is independent of temperature dependence, so both methods behave identically regardless of training data.

When the second natural frequency depends nonlinearly on temperature, both linear cointegration and Gaussian process-based nonlinear cointegration trained without nonlinear-regime data remove residual topological structure equally well.

With a nonlinear temperature dependence of the second natural frequency, Gaussian process-based nonlinear cointegration trained on data that include the nonlinear regime more effectively removes residual topological structure that linear cointegration _________(leaves/eliminates).

Assertion (A): Nonlinear temperature dependence of the second natural frequency causes linear cointegration to leave residual topological structure, while Gaussian process-based nonlinear cointegration trained on data including the nonlinear regime more effectively removes these effects. Reason (R): Residual topological structure arises solely from temperature-independent measurement noise.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which approach provides a quantitative topology-based metric for assessing the effectiveness of cointegration in detrending environmental and operational variability [A] Dynamic time warping distances between raw series magnitudes [B] Hausdorff distances between reconstructed state-space trajectories [C] Wasserstein-distance comparisons of persistent homology on Vietoris-Rips complexes of embedded series [D] Kernel density estimates of residual distributions

Assertion (A): Persistent homology comparisons using Wasserstein distances on Vietoris-Rips complexes of embedded series provide a quantitative topology-based metric for assessing the effectiveness of cointegration in detrending environmental and operational variability. Reason (R): Vietoris-Rips complexes cannot be constructed from embedded series, so topological comparisons cannot inform cointegration detrending.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which approach accurately describes inferring annual corporate ESG ratings from news using the specified temporal granularity and feature integration? [A] Use a weekly multivariate time series integrating fully supervised ESG detection, overall sentiment polarity, and named entity frequencies. [B] Use a monthly multivariate time series integrating weakly supervised ESG-relevance detection, sentiment ratios computed separately for relevant and irrelevant articles, and frequencies of semantic topic clusters. [C] Use a monthly univariate time series of average sentiment over all articles without relevance filtering or topic features. [D] Use a monthly multivariate time series of stock prices, trading volumes, and analyst ratings.
Inferring annual corporate ESG ratings from news requires daily univariate series of overall sentiment only, without ESG-relevance detection or topic cluster frequencies.

To infer annual corporate ESG ratings from news, the system models a _________(monthly multivariate time series/multi-year cross-sectional panel) that integrates weakly supervised ESG-relevance detection, sentiment ratios for relevant and irrelevant articles, and semantic topic cluster frequencies.

Assertion (A): Annual corporate ESG ratings can be inferred from news by modeling monthly multivariate time series that integrate weakly supervised ESG-relevance detection, sentiment ratios for relevant and irrelevant articles, and semantic topic cluster frequencies. Reason (R): Annual ESG ratings are determined solely by stock price movements and therefore do not depend on news-based relevance, sentiment, or topic features.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): In predicting ESG ratings, the approach that uses deep convolutional architectures to perform regression over timeseries achieves better predictive performance than alternatives; which statement aligns with the comparative finding against classification and index-aggregation baselines based on document-level transformer outputs? [A] Classification over timeseries with deep convolutional architectures beats regression and index-aggregation of document-level transformer outputs [B] Regression over timeseries with recurrent architectures outperforms classification and index-aggregation baselines based on document-level transformer outputs [C] Regression over timeseries with deep convolutional architectures outperforms both classification and index-aggregation baselines based on document-level transformer outputs [D] Index-aggregation baselines based on document-level transformer outputs are superior to regression over timeseries with deep convolutional architectures

Formulating ESG rating prediction as a regression task over timeseries with deep convolutional architectures yields _________(superior/inferior) predictive performance compared to classification and to index-aggregation baselines based on document-level transformer outputs.

Assertion (A): ESG rating prediction formulated as regression over timeseries with deep convolutional architectures outperforms both classification and index-aggregation baselines based on document-level transformer outputs. Reason (R): The approach is framed as a classification problem rather than a regression problem.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): In generating scalable ESG-relevance signals, which combination aligns with the claim that a DistilBERT-based classifier is bootstrapped under weak supervision via sentence-embedding similarity to ESG definitions and avoids manual keyword selection [A] Weak supervision; similarity to ESG definitions; DistilBERT; requires manual keyword selection [B] Weak supervision; similarity to general corpora; DistilBERT; no manual keyword selection [C] Weak supervision; sentence-embedding similarity to ESG definitions; DistilBERT; no manual keyword selection [D] Full supervision; sentence-embedding similarity to ESG definitions; LSTM; no manual keyword selection

Generating scalable ESG-relevance signals requires manual keyword selection and does not involve using sentence-embedding similarity to ESG definitions to bootstrap a DistilBERT-based classifier under weak supervision.

Scalable ESG-relevance signals are produced when a DistilBERT-based classifier is bootstrapped via sentence-embedding similarity to ESG definitions under weak supervision, while _________(avoiding/requiring) manual keyword selection.

Assertion (A): Weak supervision using sentence-embedding similarity to ESG definitions effectively bootstraps a DistilBERT-based classifier to generate scalable ESG-relevance signals without manual keyword selection. Reason (R): Manual keyword selection is essential to initialize a DistilBERT-based classifier for ESG relevance.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): For a dataset described as a large-scale news corpus aligned to thousands of US companies and annual third‑party ESG scores over multiple years and publicly released to support training and evaluation of ESG prediction models, which combination of attributes must hold? [A] News content; thousands of US companies; annual third‑party ESG scores across multiple years; public release; training and evaluation purpose [B] Social media content; hundreds of global companies; quarterly internal ESG metrics; private access; exploratory analysis only [C] News content; dozens of US companies; single-year ESG annotations from the same organization; embargoed release; training only [D] Financial report content; thousands of US companies; no ESG alignment; public release; topic modeling only

Assertion (A): A large-scale news corpus aligned to thousands of US companies and annual third‑party ESG scores over multiple years has been compiled and publicly released to support training and evaluation of ESG prediction models. Reason (R): The dataset consists solely of quarterly internal ESG metrics without any news content or multi-year coverage.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): In the setting where only a subset of variables can be specified and the remainder are randomly drawn, for popular kernels, what is optimized and what guarantees hold for posterior-sampling algorithms that use GP samples and UCB-style bonuses for empirical input-distribution estimates under an independence assumption when the input distribution may be known or unknown? [A] The marginal expectation is optimized and cumulative regret is sublinear but grows exponentially with dimension. [B] The conditional expectation over unspecified variables is optimized and Bayesian cumulative regret is sublinear for popular kernels without exponential dependence on dimensionality. [C] The worst-case value over unspecified variables is optimized and regret is constant regardless of dimension. [D] A deterministic objective is optimized and regret bounds require all variables to be specified.

Bayesian optimization with partially specified queries requires full specification of all input variables and optimizes an unconditional expectation, and posterior-sampling algorithms without independence assumptions yield regret bounds that grow exponentially with dimensionality for popular kernels.

Under an independence assumption and using GP samples with UCB-style bonuses for empirical input-distribution estimates, posterior-sampling algorithms for known and unknown input distributions in Bayesian optimization with partially specified queries—where only a subset of input variables is specified and the remainder are randomly drawn, optimizing the conditional expectation over unspecified variables—achieve _________(sublinear/linear) Bayesian cumulative regret for popular kernels without exponential dependence on dimensionality.

Assertion (A): Bayesian optimization with partially specified queries optimizes the conditional expectation over unspecified variables when only a subset of inputs is specified and the rest are randomly drawn, and posterior-sampling algorithms that incorporate GP samples with UCB-style bonuses for empirical distribution estimates under an independence assumption achieve sublinear Bayesian cumulative regret for popular kernels without exponential dependence on dimensionality. Reason (R): These guarantees hold because the algorithms require full specification of all input variables and do not address unknown input distributions.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Select the statement that accurately characterizes a boundary-aware feature propagation approach for 3D point cloud semantic segmentation in terms of its framework, joint predictions, feature propagation target, and error reduction outcome. [A] Implemented as a single-task network with no shared encoder, it predicts boundary locations and semantic labels, propagates features away from boundaries, and reduces runtime without affecting boundary errors. [B] Implemented as a multitask framework with separate encoders, it predicts only directions toward interiors, propagates features toward object centers, and reduces false positives on large objects. [C] Implemented as a multitask framework with a shared encoder, it jointly predicts object boundary locations, directions pointing toward object interiors, and semantic labels, fuses boundaries and directions to propagate features toward true boundaries, and reduces boundary-related segmentation errors. [D] Implemented for 2D image segmentation, it estimates semantic labels alone and applies a smoothing filter near edges to reduce boundary errors.

In 3D point cloud semantic segmentation, a boundary-aware feature propagation mechanism uses a single-task framework without a shared encoder, ignores boundary and direction predictions, propagates features away from true boundaries, and therefore does not reduce boundary-related segmentation errors.

For 3D point cloud semantic segmentation, the boundary-aware feature propagation mechanism operates with a _________(shared/separate) encoder in a multitask framework that jointly predicts boundary locations, interior-pointing directions, and semantic labels, fuses boundaries and directions to propagate features toward true boundaries, and reduces boundary-related segmentation errors.

Assertion (A): In 3D point cloud semantic segmentation, a boundary-aware feature propagation mechanism uses a multitask framework with a shared encoder to jointly predict boundary locations, interior-pointing directions, and semantic labels, and fuses boundaries and directions to propagate features toward true boundaries, reducing boundary-related segmentation errors. Reason (R): The framework treats boundary detection as an independent post-processing step and omits direction prediction.
output: A is True, R is False

input: A federated recommender that aggregates client-specific encoders while training only a global decoder locally enables private personalized recommendations, worsens convergence and accuracy relative to federated averaging, falls far short of centralized training, and increases computation and communication by transmitting all model parameters irrespective of rated items.

An architecture that enables private personalized recommendations, improves convergence and accuracy relative to federated averaging, and attains performance close to centralized training trains client-specific encoders locally within an autoencoder and aggregates only a _________(global decoder/global encoder) while substantially reducing computation and communication by updating and transmitting only decoder parameters associated with rated items.

Assertion (A): A federated recommender that trains client-specific encoders locally within an autoencoder and aggregates only a global decoder yields private personalized recommendations, improves convergence and accuracy over federated averaging, attains performance close to centralized training, and substantially reduces computation and communication by updating and transmitting only decoder parameters associated with rated items. Reason (R): The server aggregates full encoder and decoder parameters from every client in each round.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement best captures the relationship between the optimal untargeted perturbation and the trigger in a linear classifier with an additive patch trigger, and the resulting resemblance of inputs? [A] The perturbation has negligible projection onto the trigger direction, so adversarial and triggered inputs should not look similar. [B] The perturbation is anti-parallel to the trigger direction, making adversarial and triggered inputs dissimilar. [C] The perturbation has a substantial projection onto the trigger direction, which theoretically accounts for the similarity between adversarial and triggered inputs. [D] The perturbation aligns with random noise rather than the trigger direction, yet still explains the similarity.

In a _________(linear/nonlinear) classifier with an additive patch trigger, the optimal untargeted adversarial perturbation substantially projects onto the trigger direction, which theoretically explains the similarity between adversarial and triggered inputs.

Assertion (A): In a linear classifier with an additive patch trigger, the optimal untargeted adversarial perturbation substantially aligns with the trigger direction, accounting for the similarity between adversarial and triggered inputs. Reason (R): The trigger direction is necessarily orthogonal to any optimal untargeted perturbation in a linear model.
output: A is True, R is False

input: By associating trigger-like patterns with _________(multiple labels/a single target label), fine-tuning on untargeted adversarial examples disrupts the trigger–target correlation that drives backdoor behavior while preserving performance on clean data.

Assertion (A): Fine-tuning an infected model on untargeted adversarial examples associates trigger-like patterns with multiple labels, disrupts the trigger–target correlation that drives backdoor behavior, and preserves performance on clean data. Reason (R): This occurs because targeted adversarial examples strengthen the mapping between the trigger and one target label.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Backdoors can be erased _________(without a clean extra dataset/only with a large clean dataset) by progressively alternating adversarial fine-tuning with clean-sample identification based on logit-similarity between infected and purified models to iteratively refine both the model and the auxiliary dataset.

Assertion (A): Backdoors can be erased without a clean extra dataset by progressively alternating adversarial fine-tuning with clean-sample identification based on logit-similarity between infected and purified models to iteratively refine both the model and the auxiliary dataset. Reason (R): This is because the process relies on a fixed auxiliary dataset and standard fine-tuning that avoids adversarial examples.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which configuration and outcome correctly describe the setting in which NextG spectrum sharing benefits occur? [A] Data augmentation with GAN-generated synthetic samples combined with periodic retraining over channel coherence frames yields shorter sensing phases with low false alarm and misdetection rates, increasing user throughput and protecting incumbents under both AWGN and Rayleigh fading. [B] No data augmentation and continuous per-slot retraining yield longer sensing phases with higher false alarm rates, increasing throughput only under AWGN. [C] One-time GAN-based augmentation at deployment in static channels increases throughput by allowing higher misdetection and reduced incumbent protection. [D] Classical oversampling with periodic retraining over coherence frames provides unchanged sensing duration with low false alarms but higher misdetection under AWGN only.

In NextG spectrum sharing without periodic retraining, augmenting limited real spectrum sensing data with GAN-generated synthetic samples necessitates longer sensing phases and yields higher false alarm or misdetection rates, reducing user throughput and compromising incumbent protection only under AWGN and not Rayleigh fading.

In NextG spectrum sharing with periodic retraining over channel coherence frames, augmenting limited real spectrum sensing data with GAN-generated synthetic samples maintains low false alarm and misdetection rates despite shorter sensing phases, increasing user throughput and protecting incumbents under _________(both AWGN and Rayleigh/AWGN only) fading.

Assertion (A): In NextG spectrum sharing with periodic retraining over channel coherence frames, augmenting limited real spectrum sensing data with GAN-generated synthetic samples enables shorter sensing phases while maintaining low false alarm and misdetection rates, thereby increasing user throughput and protecting incumbents under both AWGN and Rayleigh fading. Reason (R): This is because Rayleigh fading is absent in the operating channels.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement best summarizes the relative advantages of GAN-based sensing data augmentation under Rayleigh fading versus AWGN and the impact of excessive synthetic data on classifier performance and training-data mixture? [A] Rayleigh benefits less than AWGN because Rayleigh is less variable; synthetic data never harms accuracy, so more generated data is always preferable. [B] AWGN yields greater benefit than Rayleigh because AWGN is more variable; excessive synthetic data improves accuracy, so using only generated samples is optimal. [C] Rayleigh yields greater benefit than AWGN due to higher channel variability, but relying too heavily on synthetic samples can degrade classifier accuracy, implying an optimal balance between real and generated training data. [D] AWGN and Rayleigh offer identical benefits, and the mix of real versus generated data does not influence accuracy, so no balance is needed.

GAN-based sensing data augmentation confers greater benefit under AWGN than under Rayleigh fading, and increasing the proportion of synthetic samples consistently improves classifier accuracy, implying that maximizing generated data is optimal.

GAN-based sensing data augmentation provides more benefit under Rayleigh fading than AWGN because of higher channel variability, but excessive reliance on synthetic samples can _________(degrade/improve) classifier accuracy, implying an optimal balance between real and generated training data.

Assertion (A): GAN-based sensing data augmentation is more beneficial under Rayleigh fading than AWGN, and excessive synthetic data can degrade classifier accuracy, indicating the need for a balanced mix of real and generated samples. Reason (R): AWGN has higher channel variability than Rayleigh fading.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): In MPI-parallel applications that perform file I/O, what combination of factors and underlying rationale accurately describes the determinants of checkpoint file size? [A] Algorithmic complexity and CPU frequency, because the checkpoint contains only binaries and excludes runtime buffers [B] Workload, counts of processes and nodes, and collective I/O parameters (aggregator number and aggregator buffer size), because the image includes application data, libraries, communication buffers, and an extra aggregator-concentrated I/O buffer whose size depends on the MPI-IO implementation and its two-phase optimization [C] Storage network topology alone, because MPI-IO removes intermediate buffering during two-phase operations [D] Node count and virtual memory page size, because collective I/O settings do not affect the composition or size of checkpoint images

For MPI-based parallel applications that perform file I/O, checkpoint file size is governed solely by workload and node count, because the checkpoint image excludes communication buffers and any aggregator-centered I/O buffer and is therefore independent of the MPI-IO implementation or two-phase optimization.

For MPI-based parallel applications performing file I/O, the checkpoint file size is governed by workload, process and node counts, and collective I/O configuration (number of aggregators and aggregator buffer size), because the checkpoint image includes application data, libraries, communication buffers, and an additional I/O buffer zone in aggregator processes whose magnitude depends on the MPI-IO implementation and its _________(two-phase optimization/single-phase buffering).

Assertion (A): In MPI-based parallel applications performing file I/O, checkpoint file size is governed by workload, process and node counts, and collective I/O configuration (aggregator count and aggregator buffer size). Reason (R): The aggregator-centered I/O buffer zone is uniformly distributed across all ranks and is unaffected by MPI-IO implementation or two-phase optimization.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which scenario correctly states how DNNs trained on channel state information are used, what communications are enabled, and what governs reliability in the specified channels? [A] DNNs trained on channel state information are used only for equalization, enabling IoT-uncoordinated single-RIS links that require a direct path, with reliability governed by coding rate under additive white Gaussian noise [B] DNNs trained on channel state information compute RIS element phase shifts and classify received symbols, enabling IoT-coordinated, low-complexity multi-RIS cooperative communications without a direct path, with reliability governed by relay placement under Rayleigh fading and path loss [C] DNNs trained on channel state information compute transmit beamforming while symbols are decoded separately, enabling high-complexity multi-RIS links that depend on a direct path, with reliability governed solely by transmit power in free-space conditions [D] DNNs trained on channel state information classify symbols only, enabling IoT-coordinated single-RIS links without cooperation, with reliability governed by modulation order under Rician fading

Training deep neural networks on channel state information to compute RIS element phase shifts and to classify received symbols enables IoT-coordinated, low-complexity multi-RIS cooperative communications only when a direct path exists, with reliability governed primarily by modulation order rather than relay placement under Rayleigh fading and path loss.

Training deep neural networks on channel state information to compute RIS element phase shifts and to classify received symbols enables IoT-coordinated, low-complexity multi-RIS cooperative communications without a direct path, with reliability governed by _________(relay placement/modulation order) under Rayleigh fading and path loss.

Assertion (A): Training deep neural networks on channel state information to compute RIS element phase shifts and to classify received symbols enables IoT-coordinated, low-complexity multi-RIS cooperative communications without a direct path, with reliability governed by relay placement under Rayleigh fading and path loss. Reason (R): Under Rayleigh fading and path loss, reliability is primarily determined by modulation order rather than relay placement.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): On social media depression detection tasks, which configuration yields superior and explainable performance compared with using textual or behavioral signals alone? [A] Relying solely on social interaction metrics and posting-time patterns. [B] Integrating hierarchical attention-based tweet representations with behavioral features including social interactions, posting-time patterns, emoji sentiment enriched with valence–arousal–dominance, topic distributions, and domain-specific symptom and medication cues. [C] Using only bag-of-words tweet text with topic distributions. [D] Using only emoji sentiment without valence–arousal–dominance signals combined with medication mentions.

In social media depression detection, using either textual signals alone or behavioral signals alone is superior and more explainable than integrating hierarchical attention-based tweet representations with behavioral features such as social interactions, posting-time patterns, emoji sentiment with valence–arousal–dominance, topic distributions, and symptom/medication cues.

Emoji sentiment enriched with _________(valence–arousal–dominance/part-of-speech) signals is among the behavioral features fused with hierarchical attention-based tweet representations to achieve superior and explainable depression detection on social media compared with using textual or behavioral signals alone.

Assertion (A): Integrating hierarchical attention-based tweet representations with the specified multi-aspect behavioral features provides superior and explainable depression detection on social media versus text-only or behavior-only approaches. Reason (R): Text-only hierarchical attention models capture all relevant information, making behavioral features unnecessary for performance or explainability.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement accurately describes the distribution of important and relevant skills for a specific job title across other titles? [A] They are most frequent in semantically dissimilar job titles. [B] They tend to be more frequent in semantically similar job titles. [C] They occur at equal rates regardless of semantic similarity. [D] They are exclusive to the original job title
Important and relevant skills for a specific job title tend to appear more often across semantically dissimilar job titles than across semantically similar ones.
Across job titles that are semantically similar to a particular title, the important and relevant skills for that title show _________(higher/lower) rates of occurrence.

Assertion (A): Important and relevant skills for a job title tend to appear more frequently across semantically similar job titles. Reason (R): Important and relevant skills are evenly distributed across all job titles regardless of semantic similarity.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Select the statement that correctly characterizes the input, model, and weak-label definition for inferring skill importance for a job title. [A] A LaBSE-based model trained with weak labels equal to normalized skill frequencies among semantically similar titles uses only the title as input [B] A LaBSE-based model trained with weak labels equal to raw skill counts among semantically similar titles uses the full job posting as input [C] A bag-of-words model trained with weak labels equal to normalized skill frequencies among semantically similar companies uses the title and description as input [D] A LaBSE-based model trained with expert strong labels from dissimilar titles uses only the title as input

Assertion (A): Skill importance for a job title can be inferred from the title alone by training a LaBSE-based model with weak labels defined as normalized skill frequencies within semantically similar titles. Reason (R): The approach depends on expert-generated strong labels extracted from full job descriptions.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): To improve ranking so that specialized skills are prioritized and generic skills are down-weighted, which transformation is applied to predicted skill importance? [A] Multiply by inverse document frequency of each skill. [B] Multiply by document frequency of each skill. [C] Subtract inverse document frequency from each score. [D] Normalize scores to sum to one.
To prioritize specialized skills and down-weight generic ones, predicted skill importance is _________(multiplied by inverse document frequency/multiplied by document frequency) for each skill.

Assertion (A): Applying inverse document frequency as a multiplicative factor to predicted skill importance improves specialized-skill ranking by down-weighting generic skills. Reason (R): Inverse document frequency increases with the number of documents containing the skill.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Fine-tuning a multilingual sentence encoder using only English job-title data leads to which outcome in skill importance ranking across languages? [A] Improvement limited to non-English titles with no change in English [B] Degradation for both English and non-English titles [C] Improved English results coupled with reduced performance on non-English titles [D] No trade-off; both English and non-English results improve
Fine-tuning a multilingual sentence encoder on English job-title data improves skill importance ranking performance for both English and non-English titles.

Fine-tuning a multilingual sentence encoder using English job-title data produces a trade-off: better English skill importance ranking and _________(worse non-English performance/unchanged non-English performance)

Assertion (A): Fine-tuning a multilingual sentence encoder on English job-title data yields improved English skill importance ranking alongside reduced performance on non-English titles. Reason (R): Because the model is perfectly multilingual, English-only fine-tuning cannot hurt non-English performance.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement correctly characterizes laMSD, NJS-MSE, and NFDS-MSE? [A] They are generic object detection scores that replace point error in evaluating accuracy [B] They increase numerical stability by normalizing point errors in still images [C] They quantify inter-frame jitter and sensitivity to face detector initialization in face landmark tracking, providing a robustness assessment beyond standard point error metrics [D] They estimate model parameter counts to infer generalization

The laMSD, NJS-MSE, and NFDS-MSE metrics _________(quantify/ignore) inter-frame jitter and sensitivity to face detector initialization in face landmark tracking, enabling robustness assessment beyond standard point error metrics.

Assertion (A): laMSD, NJS-MSE, and NFDS-MSE enable robustness assessment beyond standard point error metrics by quantifying inter-frame jitter and sensitivity to face detector initialization in face landmark tracking. Reason (R): They measure only average point localization error in static images.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): In face alignment, General Training eXtensions composed of geometric and photometric augmentation, statistical normalization with noise injection, initialization variation, and teacher–student domain-specific annotation yield which outcome across different algorithms and devices? [A] Improved speed only, with no change in robustness or tracking smoothness. [B] Benefits limited to a single device, improving accuracy but degrading robustness under extreme pose. [C] Improved accuracy, robustness to illumination and extreme pose, and smoother tracking across different algorithms and devices. [D] Gains achieved by replacing teacher–student annotation with unsupervised labeling, limited to one algorithm.

General Training eXtensions that include geometric and photometric augmentation, statistical normalization and noise injection, initialization variation, and teacher–student domain-specific annotation only enhance accuracy on a single face alignment algorithm and do not improve robustness to illumination or extreme pose, nor tracking smoothness across devices.

Assertion (A): General Training eXtensions comprising geometric and photometric augmentation, statistical normalization and noise injection, initialization variation, and teacher–student domain-specific annotation improve accuracy, robustness to illumination and extreme pose, and tracking smoothness across different face alignment algorithms and devices. Reason (R): Their benefits are confined to a single face alignment algorithm on one device, so cross-algorithm and cross-device gains are not observed.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement best characterizes the outcome when cascaded regression face alignment algorithms are implemented in multicore-parallel C++ with software optimizations, considering platform coverage and accuracy? [A] Real-time operation on embedded and desktop platforms is achieved without loss in landmark localization accuracy. Multicore-parallel C++ and optimized implementations of cascaded regression face alignment achieve real-time inference only on desktop systems and do so by sacrificing landmark localization accuracy.

Multicore-parallel C++ and optimized software for cascaded regression face alignment achieve real-time inference on embedded and desktop platforms _________(without degrading/while degrading) landmark localization accuracy.

Assertion (A): Multicore-parallel C++ implementations and software optimization of cascaded regression face alignment algorithms provide real-time inference on embedded and desktop platforms without degrading landmark localization accuracy. Reason (R): These implementations rely on single-core execution and attain speed by lowering landmark localization precision.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): When temporal information is exploited via filtering for still-image face landmark detectors, what is the resulting behavior with respect to stability, real-time tracking, jitter, and rapid head pose changes? [A] Reduced computational latency at the cost of higher jitter during fast head turns. [B] No change in tracking behavior because filtering applies only to depth data. [C] Conversion into stable real-time trackers that mitigate jitter and failures during rapid head pose changes. [D] Improved static-frame precision without influencing robustness to fast pose variations.
Leveraging temporal information through filtering increases jitter and failure rates for still-image face landmark detectors during rapid head pose changes while preventing real-time operation.

Leveraging temporal information through filtering converts still-image face landmark detectors into stable _________(real-time/offline) trackers that reduce jitter and failures under rapid head pose changes.

Assertion (A): Leveraging temporal information through filtering converts still-image face landmark detectors into stable real-time trackers that reduce jitter and failures under rapid head pose changes. Reason (R): Filtering discards temporal cues and enforces independent per-frame processing, which improves robustness to rapid pose changes.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement correctly combines partitioning, model choices, holiday-demand inference, and outcomes for forecasting nonlinear, intermittent restaurant sales in real time? [A] Data partitioned by day-of-week and holiday type; stacked LSTMs for ordinary days; MLPs use pre-holiday signals to infer total holiday demand and distribute it across holiday days; captures weekly seasonality and event-driven surges and is lightweight for real-time [B] Data partitioned by calendar month and weather class; MLPs model ordinary days; LSTMs estimate holiday totals from post-holiday signals; fails to capture weekly seasonality and is not real-time capable [C] No partitioning; stacked LSTMs model all days; MLPs smooth holiday periods using rolling means; captures only long-term trends and requires offline computation [D] Data partitioned by day-of-week and holiday type; stacked LSTMs for holiday days; MLPs infer ordinary-day totals from pre-holiday signals; captures daily noise but not surges and is computationally intensive

An approach that partitions restaurant sales by day-of-week and holiday type and models ordinary days with stacked LSTMs must rely on post-holiday signals to infer holiday demand and is too computationally heavy for real-time deployment, so it cannot effectively capture weekly seasonality or event-driven surges in nonlinear, intermittent demand.

An approach for restaurant sales that partitions by day-of-week and holiday type, uses MLPs to infer total holiday demand from pre-holiday signals and allocate it across holiday days, and stays efficient for real-time use while capturing weekly seasonality and event-driven surges in nonlinear, intermittent demand models ordinary days with _________(stacked LSTMs/stacked CNNs).

Assertion (A): Partitioning restaurant sales by day-of-week and holiday type, modeling ordinary days with stacked LSTMs, and using MLPs to infer and allocate total holiday demand from pre-holiday signals both captures weekly seasonality and event-driven surges in nonlinear, intermittent demand and remains computationally lightweight for real-time deployment; Reason (R): This is because a single transformer trained on unpartitioned data models all effects and computes holiday demand solely from post-holiday sales.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Identify the correct comprehensive description of a decision tree approach that maintains interpretability while reducing the training–testing performance discrepancy and improving predictive performance and stability over CART and prior soft trees by jointly optimizing splits and pruning in an end-to-end bilevel formulation, enforcing discrete paths and architecture via Gumbel-Softmax reparameterization and proximal iterations, and enabling closed-form leaf values under mean squared error and cross-entropy losses. [A] A jointly optimized bilevel tree with discrete paths via Gumbel-Softmax and proximal steps and closed-form leaves for MSE and cross-entropy that outperforms CART and soft trees and narrows the train–test gap. [B] A soft tree trained with continuous relaxations and no pruning that matches CART in stability but lacks closed-form leaf solutions for standard losses. [C] A tree that prunes post hoc after training splits, uses temperature-annealed softmax without proximal updates, and cannot guarantee interpretability or improved generalization. [D] A single-level optimization of splits only with discrete constraints that does not improve over CART and does not reduce the train–test discrepancy.

A decision tree framework relaxes paths continuously without proximal updates, lacks closed-form leaf values under mean squared error and cross-entropy, fails to maintain interpretability, increases the training–testing performance discrepancy, and does not improve predictive performance or stability relative to CART and prior soft trees.

Assertion (A): A decision tree framework achieves end-to-end bilevel optimization of splits and pruning with discrete paths and architecture via Gumbel-Softmax reparameterization and proximal iterations, closed-form leaf values under mean squared error and cross-entropy, maintained interpretability, reduced training–testing discrepancy, and improved predictive performance and stability over CART and prior soft trees. Reason (R): It relaxes tree paths continuously with a standard softmax without proximal updates, which prevents closed-form leaves and degrades interpretability.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): In wireless sensor networks with omnidirectional sensing and communication, modeling the region of interest as a circle and deterministically placing sensors on concentric rings at radii n·√3 times the sensing range with 8n evenly spaced nodes per ring leads to which overall results? [A] High coverage, reduced boundary holes compared to hexagonal tessellation, and fewer nodes than strip-based square deployments of equal area. [B] Moderate coverage, similar boundary holes to hexagonal tessellation, and the same number of nodes as strip-based square deployments. [C] High coverage, increased boundary holes compared to hexagonal tessellation, and fewer nodes than strip-based square deployments. [D] High coverage, reduced boundary holes compared to hexagonal tessellation, but more nodes than strip-based square deployments.

High coverage with reduced boundary holes versus hexagonal tessellation and fewer nodes than strip-based square layouts is achieved in omnidirectional wireless sensor networks by placing 8n evenly spaced nodes per ring on concentric rings in a circular region at radii _________(n·√3 times the sensing range/n·2 times the sensing range).

Assertion (A): In omnidirectional wireless sensor networks, modeling the region as a circle and deterministically placing sensors on concentric rings at radii n·√3 times the sensing range with 8n evenly spaced nodes per ring yields high coverage, reduces boundary holes relative to hexagonal tessellation, and requires fewer nodes than strip-based deployments over equal-area squares. Reason (R): Hexagonal tessellation minimizes boundary holes in circular regions, and strip-based square layouts are node-optimal for equal-area coverage.
output: A is True, R is False

input: A tree-based search over compositions of unary matrix primitives, guided by input-output dimensional typing constraints and a probabilistic ranking that combines function-usage priors with optional TF-IDF-based alignment of user text hints to documentation, can synthesize R code for a desired matrix transformation from a _________(single/multiple) partially specified example.

Assertion (A): A tree-based search over compositions of unary matrix primitives, guided by input-output dimensional typing constraints and a probabilistic ranking combining function-usage priors with optional TF-IDF-based alignment of user text hints to documentation, can synthesize R code that performs a desired matrix transformation from a single partially specified example. Reason (R): The approach relies solely on binary matrix primitives and excludes any use of user text hints in its ranking.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): In guiding uncertainty sampling toward the most informative criterion, how should predictive uncertainty be conceptualized and which component should be prioritized? [A] Treat predictive uncertainty as a single undifferentiated quantity and prioritize large total variance. [B] Decompose predictive uncertainty into reducible epistemic and irreducible aleatoric components, and prioritize epistemic uncertainty. [C] Decompose predictive uncertainty into reducible epistemic and irreducible aleatoric components, and prioritize aleatoric uncertainty. [D] Decompose predictive uncertainty into components without regard to reducibility, and prioritize whichever is largest.

Assertion (A): Separating predictive uncertainty into reducible epistemic and irreducible aleatoric components enables uncertainty sampling rules that target epistemic uncertainty as the more informative criterion. Reason (R): Aleatoric uncertainty is more informative because it can be eliminated with additional sampling.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Feature-wise evidence-based uncertainty measures are characterized by ignoring class priors and model aggregation and not capturing model uncertainty; under which scenario does this lead to inconsistency with predictive entropy? [A] Balanced classes with nonlinear models [B] Class imbalance and linear models [C] Noisy labels and deep ensembles [D] Uniform class priors and kernelized models

Feature-wise evidence-based uncertainty measures account for class priors and model aggregation, capture model uncertainty, and therefore remain consistent with predictive entropy under class imbalance and linear models.

Because they _________(disregard/incorporate) class priors and model aggregation and do not capture model uncertainty, feature-wise evidence-based uncertainty measures can behave inconsistently with predictive entropy under class imbalance and linear models.

Assertion (A): Feature-wise evidence-based uncertainty measures fail to capture model uncertainty, disregard class priors and model aggregation, and can be inconsistent with predictive entropy under class imbalance and linear models. Reason (R): They remain consistent with predictive entropy because they aggregate models and encode class priors.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): What comprehensive outcome is associated with applying uncertainty sampling in conjunction with Naïve Bayes? [A] Unbiased sampling that preserves true class priors and guarantees optimal decisions. [B] Sampling bias that pulls estimated class priors toward the distribution of frequently queried uncertain instances and can result in suboptimal decisions. [C] Random sampling that leaves estimated class priors unchanged while occasionally degrading decisions. [D] Bias that pushes estimated class priors toward confidently classified instances, thereby improving decision optimality.

Using uncertainty sampling with Naïve Bayes does not induce sampling bias, keeps class prior estimates aligned with the overall population rather than frequently queried uncertain instances, and ensures optimal decisions.

Under uncertainty sampling with Naïve Bayes, the estimated class priors are pulled _________(toward frequently queried uncertain instances/toward the true population distribution), which can lead to suboptimal decisions.

Assertion (A): Uncertainty sampling with Naïve Bayes induces sampling bias that skews estimated class priors toward frequently queried uncertain instances and can yield suboptimal decisions. Reason (R): Naïve Bayes automatically corrects for any selection bias when estimating class priors, keeping them aligned with the true population.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Credal uncertainty derived from probability intervals is best characterized by which statement regarding its relationships to uncertainty types and its role? [A] It correlates moderately with both epistemic and aleatoric uncertainties, serving as a general uncertainty index [B] It correlates strongly with epistemic uncertainty and poorly with aleatoric uncertainty, effectively acting as a measure of epistemic uncertainty [C] It correlates strongly with aleatoric uncertainty while being largely uncorrelated with epistemic uncertainty, effectively acting as a measure of aleatoric uncertainty [D] It shows negligible correlations with both uncertainties, making it unsuitable for measuring epistemic uncertainty

Credal uncertainty derived from probability intervals correlates strongly with aleatoric uncertainty and poorly with epistemic uncertainty, indicating it functions primarily as a measure of aleatoric uncertainty.

Assertion (A): Credal uncertainty derived from probability intervals correlates strongly with epistemic uncertainty and poorly with aleatoric uncertainty. Reason (R): It correlates strongly with aleatoric uncertainty and weakly with epistemic uncertainty.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement best characterizes the effect of weighting candidate models by relative likelihood on class support and uncertainty? [A] It yields normalized degrees of class support, decomposes total uncertainty into epistemic and aleatoric parts, and provides graded plausibility advantages over hard credal sets [B] It yields unnormalized support degrees, cannot disentangle epistemic from aleatoric uncertainty, and requires hard credal sets for plausibility [C] It provides normalized support but conflates all uncertainty into epistemic and treats credal sets as strictly superior in plausibility [D] It produces only point estimates of class labels and removes the need to compare with credal sets

Weighting candidate models by relative likelihood produces unnormalized class support that collapses epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty into a single measure and lacks any plausibility advantage over hard credal sets.

Assertion (A): Weighting candidate models by relative likelihood fails to normalize class support, cannot separate epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty, and offers no plausibility advantage over hard credal sets. Reason (R): Hard credal sets always yield more graded plausibility than any likelihood-weighted aggregation.
output: A is False, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which configuration best satisfies all of the following for image-based agglomerate fog detection—captures multi-scale global and local features, achieves effective detection, and reduces overfitting and computational cost compared to deeper models? [A] A shallow CNN employing progressively smaller convolution kernels with pooling followed by two fully connected layers [B] A deep CNN using increasingly larger kernels, no pooling, and four fully connected layers [C] A shallow CNN with fixed large kernels, no pooling, and five fully connected layers [D] A hand-crafted feature pipeline classified by an SVM without convolutional layers

Effective agglomerate fog detection in images requires a deep network with progressively larger kernels and no pooling followed by a single fully connected layer, which reduces overfitting and computational cost compared to shallower architectures.

By using progressively smaller convolution kernels with pooling and two fully connected layers, a shallow convolutional neural network effectively detects _________(agglomerate fog/edge aliasing) in images by capturing multi-scale global and local features while reducing overfitting and computational cost compared to deeper architectures.

Assertion (A): A shallow convolutional neural network with progressively smaller convolution kernels and pooling followed by two fully connected layers effectively detects agglomerate fog in images by capturing multi-scale global and local features while reducing overfitting and computational cost relative to deeper architectures. Reason (R): Deeper architectures always underfit image tasks, so shallower networks inherently generalize better and cost less in all cases.
output: A is True, R is False

input: The aggregation step that, together with sub-image partitioning, improves robustness for agglomerate fog detection by mitigating fog-free region influence compared with whole-image classification is _________(voting/averaging).

Assertion (A): Partitioning an image into sub-images and aggregating sub-classification decisions via voting improves robustness of agglomerate fog detection compared with whole-image classification. Reason (R): Voting increases the dominance of fog-free regions in the final decision.
output: A is True, R is False

input: For agglomerate fog detection, applying dropout to the fully connected layers does not outperform training without dropout in terms of generalization performance.

Assertion (A): For agglomerate fog detection, applying dropout to the fully connected layers yields better generalization performance than training without dropout. Reason (R): The enhancement occurs because the improvement is measured on training performance rather than generalization performance.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Training with synthetic fog generated using the atmospheric scattering model yields data that are insufficient to learn detectors that transfer to real-world images.

Assertion (A): Training with synthetic fog generated using the atmospheric scattering model produces data sufficient to learn detectors that transfer to real-world images. Reason (R): Detectors trained on such synthetic fog cannot be applied to real-world images.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): In defining ReliefE's feature ranking mechanism and its scalability strategy, which combination is correct? [A] Neighbor relations computed in high-dimensional instance space only; scalability via GPU-accelerated dense tensors. [B] Cosine similarity among features in the original space; scalability by sampling fewer instances. [C] Neighbor relations computed within low-dimensional manifold embeddings of the instance space and, in multi-label problems, the label space; scalability achieved through sparse-matrix computation when handling high-dimensional data. [D] Nearest-centroid distances in compressed label embeddings only; scalability via approximate nearest neighbors in the original feature space.

Assertion (A): ReliefE's feature ranking relies on neighbor relations computed in low-dimensional manifolds of instances and, in multi-label cases, labels, achieving scalability on high-dimensional data through sparse-matrix computation. Reason (R): It depends on dense covariance estimation in the original feature space.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Intrinsic dimensionality in an embedding space is obtained by computing average absolute distances to neighbors, so the embedding dimension must still be selected manually.

Estimating intrinsic dimensionality from the empirical distribution of ratios between distances to the two nearest nonidentical neighbors _________(eliminates/necessitates) manual selection of embedding dimension in an embedding space.

Assertion (A): Intrinsic dimensionality in an embedding space is obtained by leveraging the empirical distribution of ratios between distances to the two nearest nonidentical neighbors, avoiding manual embedding-dimension choice. Reason (R): Manual selection of the embedding dimension is required because nearest-neighbor distances do not provide usable statistics for dimensionality.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Select the description that matches the neighbor-selection rule during the update step and how it affects the use of k. [A] Uses a global average neighbor count and keeps a fixed k. [B] Chooses neighbors per instance based on a similarity percentile and retains a fixed k. [C] Chooses the number of neighbors adaptively per instance by finding the largest gap in the sorted distance distribution, thus obviating a fixed k hyperparameter. [D] Uses random neighbor counts per instance but still requires a fixed k for stability.

In the update step, the neighbor count is determined per instance by finding the _________(largest gap/smallest gap) in the sorted distance distribution, thereby removing the need for a fixed k hyperparameter.

Assertion (A): The update step adaptively selects the number of neighbors per instance by identifying the largest gap in the sorted distance distribution, obviating a fixed k hyperparameter. Reason (R): A globally tuned fixed k is sufficient for all instances and is derived from cross-validation.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Averaging per-neighbor updates yields stronger robustness to outliers than updating feature weights against the average representation of the selected neighbors.

Assertion (A): Relative to averaging per-neighbor updates, using the average representation of the selected neighbors to drive feature-weight updates improves robustness to outliers. Reason (R): This is because it gives greater weight to extreme neighbors than to typical ones.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): When both probabilistic matrix sparsification and representative subspace training are applied, what outcome occurs regarding embedding resources and feature ranking quality? [A] Memory costs decrease, time costs increase, and feature ranking quality improves. [B] Memory and time costs of embedding increase, but feature ranking quality is maintained. [C] Memory and time costs of embedding decrease while the quality of the resulting feature rankings is maintained. [D] Costs are unaffected, but the quality of the resulting feature rankings declines.

Together, probabilistic matrix sparsification and representative subspace training _________(reduce/increase) memory and time costs of embedding while maintaining the quality of the resulting feature rankings.

Assertion (A): Probabilistic matrix sparsification and representative subspace training reduce memory and time costs of embedding while maintaining the quality of the resulting feature rankings. Reason (R): Maintaining the quality of feature rankings requires increasing memory and time costs of embedding.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which option accurately characterizes a role-based similarity that both preserves automorphic equivalence and incorporates non-matching neighbor information by combining two averages? [A] It uses a harmonic mean over all neighbor-pair similarities without any matching, which may not preserve automorphic equivalence. [B] It forms a weighted sum over maximum matchings of out-neighbors and discards all non-matching neighbor pairs. [C] It is a convex combination of average similarities from maximum bipartite matchings of in-neighbors and from the remaining in-neighbor pairs, thereby preserving automorphic equivalence and including non-matching neighbor information. [D] It takes only the maximum similarity among matched in-neighbor pairs and ignores the rest to approximate automorphic equivalence.

By combining average similarities from maximum bipartite matchings of _________(in-neighbors/out-neighbors) and from the remaining in-neighbor pairs, the role-based similarity preserves automorphic equivalence while incorporating non-matching neighbor information.

Assertion (A): Role-based similarity is defined as a convex combination of average similarities over maximum bipartite matchings of in-neighbors and over the remaining in-neighbor pairs, thereby preserving automorphic equivalence while incorporating non-matching neighbor information. Reason (R): This holds because the measure ignores all unmatched neighbor pairs and relies exclusively on out-neighbor comparisons.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which bound and implication correctly describe the error behavior of RoleSim* after k iterations relative to the exact score? [A] The error is bounded by C·β^k with 0<β<1, enabling precomputed iteration limits for any target accuracy. [B] The error is bounded by C/k independent of β, requiring heuristic stopping rules rather than precomputed limits. [C] The error is bounded below by β^k, so no finite iteration bound can ensure a target accuracy. [D] The error is bounded by C·k^β with β>1, allowing only empirical estimation of iteration counts.

Because the error between the k-th RoleSim* iterate and the exact score decays exponentially in β, _________(a priori iteration limits for a target accuracy are obtainable/a priori iteration limits for a target accuracy are not obtainable).
output: a priori iteration limits for a target accuracy are obtainable

input: Assertion (A): The difference between the k-th iterative RoleSim* score and its exact value is bounded by an exponentially decaying term in β, which allows precomputing the number of iterations needed for a specified accuracy. Reason (R): Polynomially decaying error bounds never permit estimation of the number of iterations required for a specified tolerance.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which combined outcome is attributed to threshold-based pruning and single-source retrieval variants of RoleSim*? [A] Reduced computational time only, no accuracy guarantees, and scalability limited to small graphs. [B] Increased memory usage with provable accuracy on small graphs only. [C] Reduced computational time and memory with provable accuracy guarantees and scalability on large graphs. [D] Unchanged performance metrics with heuristic accuracy and limited scalability.

Assertion (A): Threshold-based pruning and single-source retrieval variants of RoleSim* reduce computational time and memory while providing provable accuracy guarantees and scalability on large graphs. Reason (R): They require exhaustive pairwise computations across the entire graph to ensure accuracy.
output: A is True, R is False

input: A modular, open-source framework for multivariate partial fraction decomposition combines an improved Leinartas method—with a preliminary numerator division and an algebraic-dependence step using annihilating polynomials chosen to ensure termination under graded reverse lexicographic order—with the MultivariateApart algorithm, and realizes the computation as _________(Petri-net workflows in GPI-Space/Singular/MapReduce pipelines in Hadoop) that provide intertwined parallelism across coefficients and within termwise decompositions via fork/join with early cancellations and size-aware scheduling, producing sums whose denominators are algebraically independent with common zeros and whose numerators are reduced with respect to the denominator ideal.

Assertion (A): A modular, open-source framework for multivariate partial fraction decomposition combines an improved Leinartas method with preliminary numerator division and an algebraic-dependence step using annihilating polynomials ensuring termination under graded reverse lexicographic order together with MultivariateApart, implements Petri-net workflows in GPI-Space/Singular providing intertwined coefficient- and termwise fork/join parallelism with early cancellations and size-aware scheduling, and outputs sums with algebraically independent denominators having common zeros and numerators reduced with respect to the denominator ideal. Reason (R): The system is based on Hadoop MapReduce using lexicographic-order Gröbner bases, which forces denominators to be pairwise coprime and eliminates the need for numerator reduction.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which option best summarizes the function and utility of the system: [A] A sequence-sensitive network that consumes the entire dataset to predict labels for individual points, with no claims about generalizing to unseen points within subsets or enabling data-centric downstream tasks. [B] A permutation-invariant network that, given a training subset, estimates loss values for each example without modeling the learning algorithm’s parameters, and is primarily used for data augmentation. [C] A permutation-invariant network maps a given training subset to the parameters of the model that a specified learning algorithm would produce on that subset, generalizes to subsets containing unseen points, and enables downstream tasks including data valuation, data selection, memorization assessment, and prediction calibration. [D] A network that predicts hyperparameters for any learning algorithm but requires the subset’s examples to be in a fixed order and does not support tasks like data valuation or prediction calibration.

A permutation-invariant neural network ingests the contents of a training subset to predict the _________(parameters/hyperparameters) of the model produced by a specified learning algorithm on that subset, generalizing to subsets with unseen points and enabling data valuation, data selection, memorization assessment, and prediction calibration.

Assertion (A): A permutation-invariant neural network that ingests a training subset can predict the parameters of the model produced by a specified learning algorithm on that subset, generalizes to subsets with unseen points, and enables downstream tasks such as data valuation, data selection, memorization assessment, and prediction calibration. Reason (R): Because it requires a fixed ordering of training examples and predicts only hyperparameters, it cannot generalize to subsets with unseen points or support those downstream tasks.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement best characterizes adding a global utility-consistency regularizer and a local Karush–Kuhn–Tucker optimality regularizer to the predictor’s objective? [A] It relaxes constraints so models can explore low-utility regions to avoid underfitting. [B] It enforces global smoothness while promoting oscillatory updates, reducing training error but increasing overfitting. [C] It constrains learned models to align with high utility and be near-stationary, which mitigates overfitting. [D] It removes regularization to optimize training speed, leaving overfitting unchanged.

Augmenting a predictor’s objective with a global utility-consistency regularizer and a local Karush–Kuhn–Tucker optimality regularizer leads to low-utility, non-stationary predictions and exacerbates overfitting.

By adding a global utility-consistency regularizer and a local Karush–Kuhn–Tucker optimality regularizer to the predictor’s objective, the predicted models are constrained to be high-utility and _________(near-stationary/non-stationary), which mitigates overfitting.

Assertion (A): Augmenting the predictor’s objective with a global utility-consistency regularizer and a local Karush–Kuhn–Tucker optimality regularizer constrains predicted models to be high-utility and near-stationary, thereby mitigating overfitting. Reason (R): These regularizers are designed to increase non-stationarity and direct the model toward low-utility solutions to improve generalization.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Under which conditions does the dataset-to-parameters mapping have bounded sensitivity and become efficiently approximable by ReLU neural networks? [A] Learning problems with strongly convex or smooth objectives and bounded derivatives. [B] Learning problems with arbitrary non-convex, non-smooth objectives and unbounded derivatives. [C] Only problems with discontinuous objectives and unbounded derivatives. [D] Only problems with strictly concave objectives and bounded derivatives.

For learning problems with strongly convex or smooth objectives and bounded derivatives, the dataset-to-parameters mapping has unbounded sensitivity and cannot be efficiently approximated by ReLU neural networks.

Assertion (A): For learning problems with strongly convex or smooth objectives and bounded derivatives, the dataset-to-parameters mapping has bounded sensitivity and is efficiently approximable by ReLU neural networks. Reason (R): Non-smooth objectives with unbounded derivatives guarantee that the dataset-to-parameters mapping is discontinuous and cannot be approximated by ReLU networks.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement best characterizes the formulation, solution, objectives, and constraints that achieve improved performance in SDN-enabled MEC with multiple controllers?
